q: Heat transfer and thermodynamics are two ___ branches of science.
Thermodynamics deals with equilibrium states by outlining the relationship between energy and ___.
One of the basic requirements for heat transfer is the presence of a temperature ___.
There can be no net heat transfer between two systems that are at the ___ temperature.
In solid, heat is transferred by ___ process.
In conduction process, heat energy is transferred among adjacent parts of the material without the ___ of the material itself.
The third way to transfer heat energy is by radiation process in the form of ___ waves.
In heat conduction, the individual parts of a medium do not move as a ___.
When heat is transferred via conduction, heat energy is transferred internally by vibrations of ___ and molecules.
Metals have many free electrons, which makes them generally very good ___ of heat.
The rate of heat conduction through a material of cross-sectional area $A$ is proportional to the temperature difference ___.
The rate of heat conduction is inversely proportional to the ___ $l$ of the material.
The letter $k$ is the ___ conductivity of the material.
In SI units, $k$ is measured in watt per metre per ___.
The ratio $\frac{T_2 - T_1}{l}$ is called temperature ___.
Temperature gradient is also expressed in differential form as ___.
In the one dimensional form, $\frac{H}{A}$ is heat flow per unit ___.
In the one dimensional equation, $H$ is heat ___.
This mathematical model of heat conduction was first formulated by the French physicist Jean Baptiste Joseph ___.
The negative sign in Fourier's law indicates that temperature $T$ must decrease as distance $x$ ___.
In the SI units, heat flux density is measured in watt per metre ___.
In the SI units, heat current is measured in ___.
A high value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a good thermal ___.
A low value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a poor thermal conductor or thermal ___.
According to Table 5.1, which of the following materials has the highest thermal conductivity?
According to Table 5.1, which of the following gases has a thermal conductivity of $0.026\,\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$?
Materials such as copper and silver that are good electric conductors are also good ___ conductors.
Materials such as rubber, wood, and styrofoam have ___ thermal conductivity values.
Actually, the major heat transfer in human beings is due to the flow of warm ___.
Heat convection is the flow of heat through a ___ from places of higher temperature to places of lower temperature.
In heat convection, heat is carried by the movement of the fluid ___.
In heat convection, the transfer of energy occurs between an object and its environment due to fluid ___.
Convection currents are set up in the fluid because the hotter part of the fluid is not as ___ as the cooler part.
There is an upward ___ force on the hotter fluid, making it rise.
The cooler and denser fluid ___ to replace the rising hotter fluid.
Convection occurs only in a liquid or a gas, never in a ___.
___ force is what drives natural convection.
The basic equation for the rate of convection heat transfer is known as Newton's law of ___.
In the equation $H_{\text{conv}} = q A (T_2 - T_1)$, $q$ is the convective heat transfer ___.
In SI units, $q$ is expressed as watt per metre squared per ___.
When $T_2 > T_1$, heat is gained by the object and $H_{\text{conv}}$ is ___.
When $T_2 < T_1$, heat is lost by the object and $H_{\text{conv}}$ is ___.
The household hot water system is designed based on the process of heat convection in ___.
In a hot water tank system, hot water from the boiler rises and flows into the tank where it rises to the ___.
If the convective heat transfer coefficient of air is too small to cool an object, it will require ___ convection.
Heat radiation is a transfer of heat that does not require a material ___.
Heat is transferred by thermal radiation which are ___ waves.
Energy transfer by radiation is the fastest and it suffers no attenuation in a ___.
Energy transfer by radiation travels at the speed of ___.
Thermal radiation is the form of radiation emitted by bodies because of their ___.
All bodies at a temperature above absolute ___ emit thermal radiation.
Radiation is mostly considered to be a ___ phenomenon for solids that are opaque to thermal radiation.
The radiation incident on opaque solids is usually absorbed within a few ___ from the surface.
An object which can absorb all the electromagnetic radiations falling upon it is called a ___.
The total ___ power is defined as the total radiant energy of different wavelengths emitted from unit area of a surface of a body in one second.
The total emissive power of a blackbody is directly proportional to the ___ power of absolute temperature.
The equation $\varepsilon_0 = \sigma T^4$ is known as ___ law.
The radiation emitted by all real surfaces is ___ than the radiation emitted by a blackbody at the same temperature.
Emissivity is defined as the ratio of the total emissive power of an object to that of the ___ at the same temperature and wavelength.
A black object is a good absorber and a good radiator whose emissivity is close to ___.
Thermodynamics is the study of systems involving energy in the form of ___ and work.
A good example of a thermodynamic system is gas confined by a piston in a ___.
When two systems are in thermal equilibrium, there is no net ___ between them.
The zeroth law of thermodynamics is a simple definition of ___.
The ___ is an application of the conservation energy for heat and thermodynamic processes.
$Q$ is positive if heat is added to the system, and negative if heat is ___.
$W$ is positive if work is done ___ the system.
The internal energy $U$ of a system is defined as the sum of the energies of all the ___ particles.
The internal energy of ideal gases depends only on ___, not on volume or pressure.
Heat transfer and work are both energy in ___.
A thermodynamic process may be defined as a change from one ___ state of a system to another.
A ___ process happens slowly enough for the system to remain in internal physical thermodynamic equilibrium.
An ___ process occurs at constant pressure.
An example of an isobaric process is a gas slowly heated or cooled, confined by a piston in a ___.
In an isobaric process the pressure is constant and the work is equal to the area of the shaded rectangle as shown in ___ graph.
An ___ process occurs at constant volume.
When the volume of a thermodynamic system is constant, ___ is done by the system.
According to the first law, for an isochoric process, the change in internal energy must equal any ___ flowing into or out of the system.
For an ideal monatomic gas, the specific heat capacity $C_V$ is equal to ___.
An ___ process occurs at a constant temperature.
For a process to be isothermal, any heat flow into or out of the system must occur ___.
For an ideal gas, internal energy $U$ is only a function of the ___.
In an isothermal process, since $\Delta U = 0$, according to the first law, ___.
In an ___ process, there is no heat transfer into or out of the system.
An adiabatic process can be done either by enclosing the system in a ___ or by carrying out the process so quickly.
The first law of thermodynamics $\Delta U = Q - W$ is reduced to ___ in an adiabatic process.
In the expression $W = \frac{1}{\gamma-1}(p_i V_i - p_f V_f)$, $\gamma$ is the ratio of the ___.
Heat engine is a system that converts heat to ___, which can then be used to do mechanical work.
A series of processes in which a system is brought back to its initial state is called a ___.
The purpose of a heat engine is to deliver ___ continuously to the outside by performing the same cycle over and over again.
Thermal efficiency of a heat engine is the ratio of ___ to heat input.
Applying the first law of thermodynamic to one complete cycle, remembering that there is no net change of internal energy ($\Delta U = 0$), we get ___.
There are two types of internal combustion engine (gasoline engine and diesel engine) that generates the input heat by the combustion of fuel ___ the engine itself.
In the gasoline engines, the combustion of the gasoline and air takes place explosively by an ___.
The gasoline engine is called a ___ stroke engine.
A mixture of gasoline vapour and air is drawn into the cylinder by the ___ stroke of the piston.
The mixture of gasoline vapour and air is compressed until its pressure and temperature rise considerably. This is accomplished by the ___ of the piston.
Combustion of the hot mixture is caused to take place very rapidly by an electric spark. The volume remains ___ during this process.
The hot combustion products expand and push the piston out, thus suffering a drop in ___ and temperature.
An exhaust valve allows some gas to escape until the pressure drops to that of the ___.
Theoretical efficiency of a gasoline engine is $\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{(V_1/V_2)^{\gamma-1}}$, where $V_1/V_2$ is the ___.
For a typical compression ratio of 8 and with $\gamma = 1.4$, $\eta$ = ___ for an ideal gasoline engine.
Unlike the spark-ignition gasoline engine, the diesel engine induces only ___ into the combustion chamber on its intake stroke.
Diesel fuel is sprayed into the cylinder by the ___ and immediately ignites.
In a diesel engine, diesel fuel immediately ignites because of the ___ inside the cylinder.
The pressure created by the combustion of the fuel drives the piston ___.
As the piston nears the bottom of its stroke, all of the ___ open.
As the piston is at the bottom, it uncovers the ___ ports.
Pressurized air fills the cylinder, forcing out the remainder of the ___.
The exhaust valves close and the piston starts traveling back upward, re-covering the intake ports and compressing the fresh air. This is the ___ stroke.
What is the primary reason the efficiency of real engines is reduced?
a: Friction and energy losses by conduction
